text: This is a video presentation of a laparoscopic diverticulectomy of perforated duodenal diverticulum from the Mount Sinai Hospital in New York. The authors have no disclosures. The duodenum is the most common place for diverticula after the colon. The prevalence is as high as twenty-two percent in autopsy series. Perforations of the duodenal diverticula are rare and present with vague and non-specific symptoms. Cases with large perforation and or systemic compromise need emergency surgery.

Our patient is a sixty years old female with history of hypothyroidism and an endoscopic center diverticulaectomy in two thousand thirteen. She presented with vague diffuse abdominal pain associated with nausea and emesis. On physical exam she got normal vital signs and a normal laboratory profile including a normal white blood count. This picture shows an esophagram with the center diverticulum managed with endoscopic diverticulaectomy in two thousand thirteen. In the emergency department, the patient had a CT scan demonstrating free air in the retrobatonium area surrounding the area of the second and third portion of the duodenum. The patient was taken emergently to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table.

The surgeon accesses the abdominal cavity using a Hasson port, along with two additional 5mm ports in the right upper quadrant and a 12mm port in the left upper quadrant. An Ethensor retractor is employed to retract the liver, providing a clear view of the surgical field.

During the exploration of the abdominal cavity, significant inflammation and edema are observed around the second and third portions of the duodenum. The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Cocker maneuver using a high-energy device to mobilize the duodenum. A mass of tissue is noted adjacent to the duodenum, extending from the bile duct toward the third portion of the duodenum. The duodenal diverticulum is plastered to the duodenum, with its apex bent toward the bile duct. The surgeon carefully dissects the diverticulum circumferentially, revealing a perforation near its base. The dissection continues around the most cephalic aspect of the diverticulum near the gallbladder, exposing the perforation and its contents, and proceeds to the most caudal aspect using the high-energy device.

The surgeon mobilizes the diverticulum and duodenum, ensuring the head of the pancreas is visible. The diverticulum is dissected from the lateral aspect of the duodenum, revealing its base. The neck of the diverticulum is identified, and a 60mm mechanical stapler is used to perform the diverticulectomy.

The specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity, and the surgical area is inspected. A leak test using saline and air shows no evidence of leakage from the staple line. A drain is placed in the subhepatic area. The upper GI series post-operation shows no evidence of leakage.
